summary: I was born in Hiroshima Prefecture on February 14, 1890. My father was a farmer growing rice and wheat there. I left Hiroshima in 1905, right after the Russo-Japanese War ended, to come to America.

I left Japan in 1905 just after the Russo-Japanese War ended. I remember seeing Russian prisoners of war when I went to Yokohama before leaving. The war's end was a significant event that coincided with my departure to America.

Before I left Japan in 1905, I passed through Yokohama. I recall seeing Russian prisoners of war there after the Russo-Japanese War. Yokohama was a busy port where many Japanese were preparing to leave for America.

When I first came to America, I worked in Hawaii for eight months on a sugar plantation. I was only 15 years old and worked under a Chinese boss for $18 a month. Although many Japanese lived there, I found Hawaii too small and noisy, so I moved on to the mainland.

I arrived in San Francisco on June 20, 1906, just after the big earthquake. Hotels were full, and there were no regular beds available. I stayed only a few days at a Japanese hotel before heading to the countryside with my friend.

I came to San Francisco right after the 1906 earthquake. The city was in chaos, and there were no regular beds in hotels. It was a difficult time to arrive, but soon after I moved on to work in rural areas.

After San Francisco, I went to Walnut Grove where my friend's uncle lived. The area had many Chinese residents and gambling houses that were busy even on weekdays. We stayed there about a month pulling weeds on bean fields and slept in a barn.
I moved to Lodi after Walnut Grove but found no work there. So I continued on to Marysville to find employment. Lodi was just a brief stop in my journey to find steady work.

In Marysville, I worked on railroad construction for the Utah Construction Company. I remember camping near a river where heavy rain caused flooding, forcing us to take a long detour. It was hard work but a necessary step in establishing myself in America.

I was employed by the Utah Construction Company while working on railroad construction near Marysville. The work was tough, and I recall our camp being flooded by heavy rain. This job was one of my early experiences in America.

I worked as a schoolboy in Sacramento to improve my English and earn money. I also did jobs like waiter, dishwasher, and cook while living there. Sacramento was a place where I learned more about American life before settling down.
After I got married, my wife and I lived in Reedley. Before marriage, I had lived in Clovis for four years working and farming. Reedley became our home as we raised our family and farmed land.

I lived in Clovis for four years before I got married. During that time, I worked for two years and then farmed 60 acres with a partner. I also served as a Japanese foreman on a 320-acre ranch, which was challenging given my young age.

I was involved with the Japanese Association around the time of Pearl Harbor. Our district's staff members were spared arrest, possibly because Rep. Gerhardt and Mr. Setsu Sakamoto vouched for us. The association helped maintain peace and represented the community during difficult times.

Setsu Sakamoto was the director of the Japanese Association and a friend of Rep. Gerhardt. His letter to the president helped prevent the arrest of our district's Japanese Association staff after Pearl Harbor. He played a key role in protecting our community during the war.

Rep. Gerhardt was a friend of Setsu Sakamoto and supported the Japanese Association during WWII. He wrote a letter to the president assuring that the association would not oppose America, which helped prevent arrests in our district. His support was crucial for our community's safety.

I first heard about the Pearl Harbor attack while I was fishing, though I don't remember the exact details. After the attack, I was president of a strawberry growers' association and had to manage the growers under difficult restrictions. The event marked the beginning of many hardships for Japanese Americans like me.

Before evacuation, I sold the rights of the strawberry growers' association to the Pacific Fruit Company. This was during a time when travel was restricted and financial transactions were tightly controlled. The company helped manage the growers' interests during the war.

On April 18, 1942, I entered the assembly center at the Fresno Fairgrounds as part of the forced evacuation. Many Japanese from the area were gathered there before being sent to camps. It was a difficult time leaving our homes and possessions behind.

During the evacuation, people living on the west side of Highway 99 had to go to the assembly center first, while those on the east side were initially free zone. Eventually, everyone had to leave. The highway marked a dividing line in how the evacuation was enforced locally.

In Hawaii and California, I often saw Chinese people working alongside Japanese and Koreans, though living quarters were separate. After the war, when I stopped at a Chinese restaurant, I was refused service because I was Japanese. Our relations were limited and sometimes strained due to wartime tensions.

In the sugar plantation camps in Hawaii, Korean people worked alongside Japanese and Chinese, but the living quarters were separated. I did not have much direct interaction with them. The communities coexisted but remained distinct.

I did not have much dealings with Filipino people during my time in America. They were part of the broader immigrant community, but our paths rarely crossed. I mainly interacted with Japanese, Chinese, and Koreans in work and social settings.

After Pearl Harbor, I was prepared for arrest because of my role in the Japanese Association, but I was spared. Eventually, I was forced to leave my home and entered the Fresno assembly center in April 1942. Later, I was sent to the Jerome War Relocation Center in Arkansas, where life was very cold and difficult.

I was sent to the Jerome War Relocation Center in Arkansas in October 1942. The place was very cold, and the journey took four days by train under armed guard. Almost all Japanese from Fresno city went there, and my wife, brother, and sister went with me.

After the war, I traveled back through Denver. When I stopped at a Chinese restaurant there, I was refused service because I was Japanese. This showed that discrimination persisted even after the war ended.

I bought land in 1919 but could not put it in my own name, so I purchased it under a Nisei's name. Nisei, being second-generation Japanese Americans, sometimes had more legal rights. This was a common practice among Issei like me to circumvent land ownership restrictions.

After Pearl Harbor, staff members of the Judo Association were arrested, unlike those of the Japanese Association. I was not directly involved in Judo, but I knew that the Judo and Kendo associations faced harsher treatment during the war. It was a difficult time for those involved in martial arts organizations.

Similar to Judo, the Kendo Association's staff members were arrested after Pearl Harbor. I was spared because I was with the Japanese Association, but those connected to Kendo were not so fortunate. The war brought suspicion on martial arts groups.

We wanted our children to learn Japanese, so we built Japanese language schools in about 11 communities. The schools met once a week on Saturdays and hired teachers to teach the language. It was important to us to preserve our culture despite living in America.

When I was in Hawaii, I learned English by attending a Japanese church where Rev. Rokuro Abe taught. He helped us with interpreting and negotiating. Later, I met him again after he came to America, and he was a significant figure in our community.
